title: Quote Management
description: Learn the quote lifecycle, quote rules, and why proper quote management and versioning matters.

sections.title: What Is a Quote?
sections.content:
A Quote is a formal proposal. It shows what you're selling, at what price, and under what terms. It's not a binding contract—it's the bridge between solution design and legal commitment.

Quotes document the commercial deal so both you and your customer are clear on what's being offered and what it costs.

sections.title: The Core Principle: Always From Opportunity
sections.content:
Quotes are created from Opportunities. They inherit Account, Contact, and Opportunity details.

Quotes aren't standalone documents. They live within opportunities and pull key information from them. This keeps everything linked and auditable.

sections.title: Six Essential Rules
sections.content:
1. Born from Opportunity
Create quotes when the Opportunity is in Solution Definition or Pricing & Quotation. The quote inherits Customer (Account + Contact) and Opportunity details. You fill in products, quantities, and pricing.

2. One Active Quote at a Time
Typically one primary quote per Opportunity is being evaluated by the customer. New versions can be created, but previous versions stay in the record for audit and history.

3. Line Items = Specificity
Each line item is one product/service with quantity, unit price, and total. They roll up to contract value. Prices inherit from your catalog but can be adjusted (with approval for discounts).

4. Approval Before Send
No quote reaches a customer without internal sign-off. Large discounts or special terms need manager or director approval. The record shows who approved it and when.

5. Status Lifecycle
Quotes flow through clear statuses: Draft → In Review → Approved → Sent → Accepted/Rejected/Expired. Status tracks where each quote is in its journey.

6. Versioning Creates Audit Trail
When the customer asks for changes, create a new version. Don't edit the original. This keeps a clean, defensible history of every proposal you made at every negotiation stage.

sections.title: Quote to Contract: The Handoff
sections.content: When a customer accepts a quote and the deal moves to closure, the quote links to the Contract. The Contract pulls pricing, products, and terms from the quote and adds legal language. This keeps your commercial and legal agreements aligned.

sections.title: When You Need Quotes
sections.content:
Standard Proposal
Customer defined needs. You designed the solution. You quote products, quantities, and pricing for their review.

Negotiated Pricing
Customer wants volume discounts or service credits. New version, new pricing, internal approval, sent back.

Pilot/POC
Complex deal? Start small with a pilot quote. After success, create a larger production quote.

Multi-Year or Renewal
Quote documents the multi-year commitment, renewal terms, pricing, and service levels.

sections.title: Why Versioning Is Critical
sections.content:
Audit Trail
Every version is tracked. If disputes arise later, you have clear evidence of what was offered when.

Negotiation Clarity
Both you and the customer know which version is current. No confusion. No accidental old proposals.

Data Integrity
Versioning preserves complete history. Version 1 was $500K. Version 2 was $750K with added scope. This matters for forecasting and post-deal analysis.

Revenue Recognition
Accounting uses the final accepted version for revenue records. Earlier versions don't factor into financials.

Communication
Say "Quote Version 3" and everyone's on the same page. Prevents miscommunication.

sections.title: The Approval Workflow
sections.content:
1. Sales Rep Creates Draft
Select products. Enter quantities and pricing. Mark as Draft.

2. Internal Review
Account manager or product specialist checks for accuracy and completeness.

3. Pricing Approval
Large discounts or special terms? Route to Pricing Approver (sales manager or finance).

4. Final Approval
Deal size or complexity warrant it? Director, finance manager, or executive signs off.

5. Send to Customer
Mark as Approved. Send formal PDF or email.

6. Customer Review & Negotiation
They review. They ask questions. They request changes.

7. Revise & Re-approve
New version. Internal review and approval again. Send it out.

8. Acceptance
Customer agrees. Mark as Accepted. Opportunity moves to closure.

sections.title: Best Practices for Clean Quotes
sections.content:
- Always create from Opportunity, never standalone.
- Never send without internal approval.
- Use descriptive names: "Acme Corp - Financial Services Platform - Version 2" not "Quote V2."
- Write clear terms and conditions so scope and pricing are unambiguous.
- Mark old versions as Superseded when you create new ones.
- Once sent to customer, only managers can modify—no reps changing quotes after delivery.
- Always track which version was accepted—this is your revenue and contract foundation.

Quotes done right protect both you and your customer. Manage carefully. Approve thoroughly. Version thoughtfully.

keyTakeaways: Quotes are created from Opportunities and represent proposals, not binding agreements; versioning tracks negotiation history
Quotes require internal approval (pricing authority) before sending to customers, preventing errors and unauthorized commitments
Version control creates audit trails and ensures clarity about which pricing and terms are current vs. superseded
